text: The presence of nitrate ions in high concentrations in water systems poses a threat to environmental, as well as human health, necessitating the need for efficient removal approaches. Single atom alloys (SAAs) has been identified as a suitable bimetallic material in form of nanoparticles, that work synergistically as catalysts in thermocatalytic and electrocatalytic reduction of nitrate reaction. Interestingly, this study shows that the thermocatalytic (T-NRR) and electrocatalytic (E-NRR) reduction pathways occur through distinct mechanisms. Such mechanisms dictate the activities and selectivities that are exhibited by different SAAs, in this case various Pd different Pd/Cu nanoalloys with varying Pd-Cu ratios from 1:100 up to 100:1 that were synthesized using the ‘one-pot’ method were employed in E-NRR; interestingly enough, the Pd/Cu<sub>(1:100)</sub> SAA had the highest turnover frequency (TOF<sub>Pd</sub> = 2 min<sup>-1</sup>) and also the highest N<sub>2</sub> selectivity (94 %) while on the other hand the same SAA structure performed poorly towards T-NRR compared to the other nanoalloys, using the same preparation method and structural analysis to verify that SAAs were produced. Density functional theory calculations revealed that the E-NRR of Pd/Cu<sub>(1:100)</sub> SAA was more stable, giving higher N<sub>2</sub> selectivity than T-NRR due to the presence of NO<sub>3</sub>* intermediate in the E-NRR and lower barrier energy to produce N<sub>2</sub> than that of NH through localized pH and the ability of SAA catalysts to extracted protons from water.
